summary: DHH reflects on the unintended consequences of government antitrust action against Big Tech. While he has long advocated for breaking Apple and Google's app store monopolies, he now worries that the EU's Digital Markets Act and similar efforts may not disband concentrated power but rather transfer it to governments eager to control speech and online platforms. He draws parallels between Apple's frustrating experience with vague DMA compliance rules and the arbitrary app review process developers have endured for years. Ultimately, he finds himself genuinely torn — unable to fully endorse either government intervention or the status quo, concluding with a Kierkegaard-inspired admission that both paths lead to regret.
key_points: The EU's DMA is giving Apple a taste of its own medicine — vague rules, arbitrary enforcement, and post-hoc compliance judgments mirror what developers face in app store review
Government antitrust action risks not dismantling Big Tech's power but co-opting it, deputizing platforms as enforcement arms for speech regulation and censorship
Political overreach justified by one side inevitably gets turned against them when power changes hands — emergency powers and speech restrictions always expand
The Microsoft antitrust precedent is ambiguous: market disruption from Firefox, Chrome, and mobile may have mattered more than DOJ intervention
Both tackling and ignoring Big Tech monopolies carry serious risks, making this a genuinely difficult question without a clear principled answer
money_quotes: My concern is rather that the sovereign leviathans of the world, be it the EU or the US, might not exactly share as many interests with free market advocates as it appears on the surface.
Welcome to our world, Apple. This is exactly what it feels like to be a developer knocking on the door of your app store bureaucracy.
If you give up on hope, you're bound to become cynical, and that's a curse worse than any commercial dispossession.
The DMA is convoluted and complicated because the EU is trying to have its cake and eat it too.
Every overreach you find justified when its your team wielding the advantage is one you'll rue when it's turned back against you.

A future where this intolerable concentration of power is not so much disbanded as simply subsumed into the growing arsenal of oppressive powers that are increasingly being collected by Western democracies.
Tackle Big Tech, and you will regret it. Don't tackle Big Tech, and you will regret that too. Either way, you will regret it.
key_insight: Government antitrust action against Big Tech may not liberate the market but instead transfer monopoly power to states eager to weaponize platforms for speech control and censorship.
